questions.text: What does 'Proto-industrialisation' refer to?
options: The period of large scale factory production
The phase of industrialisation before the coming of factories
questions.explanation: Before factories, there was large-scale industrial production for an international market, controlled by merchants and based in households.

questions.text: In the proto-industrial stage, where was most of the production carried out?
questions.explanation: Merchants in towns moved to the countryside, supplying money to peasants and artisans to produce for them.

questions.text: Who was a 'Stapler' in the textile industry?
options: A person who 'staples' or sorts wool according to its fibre
A merchant who sold finished goods
questions.explanation: Staplers were part of the early production process in the woollen industry.

questions.text: The earliest factories in England came up in the:
questions.explanation: Factory production began early in the 18th century, but multiplied significantly in the late 18th century.

questions.text: The first symbol of the new era of industrialisation was:
questions.explanation: Cotton was the leading sector in the first phase of industrialisation in Britain.

questions.text: Who invented the Spinning Jenny in 1764?
questions.explanation: The Spinning Jenny speeded up the spinning process and reduced labour demand.

questions.text: Who improved the steam engine produced by Newcomen and patented the new engine in 1781?
questions.explanation: James Watt's steam engine was a revolutionary power source for industries.

questions.text: In Victorian Britain, why did the upper classes prefer things produced by hand?
questions.explanation: Hand-made products were individual, unique, and carefully finished, unlike mass-produced machine goods.

questions.text: Why were merchants in Britain reluctant to use machines in the early 19th century?
options: Machines were too expensive and often broke down
There was a shortage of human labour
questions.explanation: Repairing machines was costly and labor was abundant and cheap, making machines less attractive initially.

questions.text: In the mid-eighteenth century, before the machine age, which country dominated the international market in textiles?
questions.explanation: Silk and cotton goods from India were famous for their quality and fineness.

questions.text: Which Indian ports were the main centers of pre-colonial vibrant sea trade?
questions.explanation: Surat on the Gujarat coast and Masulipatnam on the Coromandel coast were major trade links.

questions.text: The paid servant appointed by the East Company to supervise weavers and collect supplies was the:
questions.explanation: Gomasthas were used to eliminate the existing system of brokers and have direct control over the weavers.

questions.text: Why did the export of Indian textiles decline by the mid-19th century?
options: British manufacturers pressurized the government to impose import duties on Indian cloth
Cotton was no longer grown in India
questions.explanation: Machine-made British textiles were cheaper and flooded the Indian market, while exports from India were blocked.

questions.text: The first cotton mill in India was established in ______ in 1854.
questions.explanation: Bombay became the center of the cotton textile industry in India.

questions.text: The first jute mill in India was set up in 1855 at Rishra in:
questions.explanation: Bengal became the hub for jute production and export.

questions.text: Who established the first Indian jute mill in Calcutta in 1917?
questions.explanation: Seth Hukumchand, a Marwari businessman, was a pioneer in the jute industry.

questions.text: Dwarkanath Tagore was a famous Indian industrialist who invested in:
questions.explanation: Tagore was one of the early entrepreneurs who traded with China and set up joint-stock companies.

questions.text: Where did most of the workers in the Bombay cotton mills come from?
questions.explanation: Most industrial workers were peasants who came from nearby districts to work in cities.

questions.text: A 'Jobber' was a person who:
options: Got new recruits for the factories
questions.explanation: Jobbers were old and trusted employees who helped workers find jobs and housing, often exerting authority.

questions.text: During the First World War, Indian industries boomed because:
options: British factories were busy producing for the war
Import of British goods into India declined
Indian factories got orders for war supplies like jute bags and uniforms
questions.explanation: The war created a vacuum in the Indian market that local industries filled.

questions.text: After the First World War, why could Britain never recapture its old position in the Indian market?
options: Britain could not compete with Japan
Indian industry had consolidated its position
questions.explanation: The war significantly shifted the global economic balance.

questions.text: Large scale industries formed only a small segment of the Indian economy. About 67% were concentrated in:
questions.explanation: Industrial development was geographically uneven in colonial India.

questions.text: The use of the 'fly shuttle' in the early 20th century helped:
options: Weavers to increase productivity and handle wide looms
questions.explanation: Technology like the fly shuttle allowed handloom weavers to compete better with mill-made cloth.

questions.text: Advertisements became a way to create a market for products. Images of ______ were often used on labels to show quality.
questions.explanation: Divine images gave divine approval to the products being sold.

questions.text: Manchester labels on cloth bundles exported to India carried the words:
questions.explanation: The label was a mark of quality and brand identity for British manufacturers.

questions.text: The term 'Orient' refers to:
options: The countries to the East of the Mediterranean (Asia)
questions.explanation: It is a traditional term used by Westerners to describe Asian cultures and regions.

questions.text: Which of the following was NOT a problem faced by Indian weavers in the 19th century?
options: High demand for their products abroad
questions.explanation: Demand for Indian hand-made textiles actually collapsed in international markets.

questions.text: The TISCO (Tata Iron and Steel Company) began producing steel in:
questions.explanation: TISCO was established by Jamsetji Tata in Jamshedpur.

questions.text: Guilds were powerful associations of:
questions.explanation: Guilds regulated competition, prices, and entry of new people into the trade.

questions.text: Industrialisation in Britain was primarily driven by which two sectors in the first and second phases?
questions.explanation: Cotton led until 1840s, followed by Iron and Steel due to the expansion of railways.

questions.text: The introduction of the Spinning Jenny led to:
options: Attacks on machines by women who feared losing jobs
questions.explanation: Technological change often faced resistance from those whose livelihoods were threatened.

questions.text: The East India Company established political power to:
options: Ensure a regular supply of raw cotton and silk
questions.explanation: Control over territories allowed the Company to monopolize trade.

questions.text: Why did the production of handloom cloth actually expand in the 20th century despite mill competition?
options: Weaving of complicated designs that machines couldn't do
Demand for coarse cloth among poor
questions.explanation: Handloom weaving survived by adapting technology and focusing on niche markets.

questions.text: The 'Age of Industrialisation' is also an age of:
questions.explanation: Industrialisation transformed every aspect of human life.

questions.text: Who was the 'Carder' in the textile process?
options: A person who prepared cotton or wool for spinning
questions.explanation: Carding is the process where fibres are disentangled and prepared for the next stage.

questions.text: Fuller was a person who:
questions.explanation: Fulling was a finishing stage in textile production.

questions.text: What does the cover of the music book 'Dawn of the Century' (1900) celebrate?
questions.explanation: The image depicted an angel of progress with symbols of railway, camera, and machines.

questions.text: Why did industrial production in India remain mostly in the small-scale sector?
options: Most production was done in small workshops and households
questions.explanation: Even in the late colonial period, factory production accounted for only a small share of the total workforce.

questions.text: When Indian manufacturers advertised, the nationalist message was often:
options: If you care for the nation, then buy Indian (Swadeshi)
questions.explanation: Advertisements became a vehicle for the nationalist message of self-reliance.

questions.text: The expansion of railways in India after 1850s led to an increase in demand for:
questions.explanation: Railways were the major consumers of heavy industrial products.
